The Trademark Statute of the People's Republic of China (中华人民共和国商标法) establishes broad rules on regulation of trademarks, defending trademark holders' exclusive rights and upholding the standard of offerings or amenities bearing the registered trademarks, seeking to ensure consumer interests and to encouraging the expansion of the socialist market economy [ref].

The Trademark Code of the People's Republic of China (中华人民共和国商标法) stipulates fundamental directives on supervision of trademarks, preserving trademark possessors' exclusive entitlements and continuity of the caliber of products or provisions marked with the registered trademarks, with the purpose of defending consumer interests and to supporting the advancement of the socialist market economy [ref].

The Trademark Act of the People's Republic of China (中华人民共和国商标法) outlines overall principles on management of trademarks, safeguarding trademark proprietors' exclusive rights and preservation of quality of goods or services carrying the registered trademarks, aiming at safeguarding consumer interests and to advancing the growth of the socialist market economy [ref].

Based on qualitative information obtained from three rural South Sudanese communities—comprising interviews and focus groups with 94 study participants, and more than five hours of community radio content—this research shows that gender narratives viewed as conventional by the South Sudanese, during a post-conflict transition period, do not inevitably form barriers to promoting gender and development targets.

Relying on qualitative material collected from three rural South Sudanese communities—including interviews and focus group discussions with 94 research participants, and over five hours of local radio programming—this study reveals that gender discourses considered customary by the South Sudanese, during a post-conflict transitional phase, do not always serve as hindrances to the realization of gender and development aims.

Utilizing qualitative insights acquired from three rural South Sudanese communities—featuring interviews and focus group sessions with 94 research participants, and over five hours of communal radio material—this investigation indicates that gender narratives perceived as traditional by the South Sudanese, throughout a post-conflict transition sequence, do not necessarily present barriers to the fulfillment of gender and development missions.

Drawing on qualitative evidence gathered from three rural South Sudanese communities—encompassing interviews and group discussions with 94 research participants, and more than five hours of community radio broadcasts—this study illustrates that gender narratives regarded as traditional by the South Sudanese, during a post-conflict transition era, do not invariably act as obstacles to the progress of gender and development objectives.

Psychological disorders were still correlated with psychological maltreatment (RR = 3.0; 95%CI 1.9-4.7 and RR = 1.8; 95%CI 1.0-3.7 in the last 12 months, and seven years, respectively), even without physical or sexual maltreatment.

Mental illnesses remained linked with psychological abuse (RR = 3.0; 95%CI 1.9-4.7 and RR = 1.8; 95%CI 1.0-3.7 in the last 12 months, and seven years, respectively), even in the absence of physical or sexual abuse.
